answer.rationale: The single explicit doctrinal statement in the New Testament Epistles on Spirit baptism is 1 Cor 12:13: en heni pneumati hemeis pantes eis hen soma ebaptisthemen (G907, aorist passive, 1st person plural), 'in one Spirit we all were baptized into one body'. Paul names this as a past-tense corporate event true of ALL believers (pantes G3956) at incorporation into the body of Christ, not as a subsequent post-conversion crisis. Eph 4:5 names hen baptisma (G908) singular, in the same listing as one Lord and one faith, which the apostle treats as foundational not optional. Rom 8:9 names the lexical line cleanly: ei de tis Pneuma Christou ouk echei houtos ouk estin autou, if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ that one does not belong to him. Possession of the Spirit and belonging to Christ are co-extensive. However the Acts narratives (2:1-4, 8:14-17, 10:44-48, 19:1-7) present a series of episodes where Spirit-reception is temporally and visibly distinct from initial faith, in some cases (Samaria, Ephesus) clearly subsequent. The honest verdict: 1 Cor 12:13 settles the doctrinal locus that every believer is Spirit-baptized into the body at incorporation into Christ, and the Acts narratives are transitional and salvation-historical (the Spirit demonstrably extending across covenantal boundaries: Jews, Samaritans, God-fearers, John's-baptism disciples) rather than templates for a normative two-stage post-conversion crisis experience. The Pauline doctrinal didactic outranks Lukan narrative description on the question of normativity.
answer.notes: This is an in-house Christian disagreement on the timing and structure of Spirit-reception, not a gospel-stake boundary. Public-record carriers of the subsequent-experience reading: classical Pentecostal lineages descending from the Azusa Street revival (1906), the Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths Article 7-8, the International Pentecostal Holiness Church, the Foursquare Gospel, and broader charismatic streams. These bodies confess the deity of Christ, the bodily resurrection, and the Trinity, and the major creeds cleanly. The dispute is real and lexically grounded on both sides: cessationist Reformed/Baptist/Brethren read 1 Cor 12:13 as the doctrinal locus and treat Acts as transitional; classical Pentecostals read Acts 2/8/10/19 as the normative pattern. Children CAN safely be taught the Pauline didactic baseline, and a continuationist family-of-origin or church that loves Christ is not a cult on this point. Where the doctrine crosses into cult-grade territory is only the further claim that without tongues a person is not regenerate at all (this position is rejected even by the AG; their official wording is tongues as initial physical evidence of the Spirit-baptism, not as a salvation test). William Branham's Latter Rain stream, the Oneness Pentecostal denial of the Trinity, and the New Apostolic Reformation's prophetic-apostolic governance claims fail other tests but not this one. The would_marry_if_held_otherwise stands true because the disagreement is non-gospel-essential; the would_be_member_otherwise stands false because the practical question of whether tongues-evidence is taught as normative directly affects discipleship of one's own family.

stem_audit.neutralized_form: When does Spirit baptism occur, at conversion as a once-for-all incorporation into Christ, or as a subsequent distinct crisis experience after the new birth?
stem_audit.notes: The statement as written loads toward the cessationist/Reformed-Baptist conclusion by naming the verdict (a once-for-all initiation rather than a separable, subsequent crisis experience) in its own stem. The neutralized form lets the lexical evidence settle the timing question on its own. The verdict still lands at conversion, but flagged for stem-pre-loaded-verdict so trusted-elder reviewers know the question was worked on its neutralized form.

lay_summary.reasoning: The Bible teaches that when a person trusts Christ, the Holy Spirit places that person into the body of Christ at that moment. The strongest reason for this is 1 Corinthians 12:13, where Paul writes 'in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, slaves or free, and all were made to drink of one Spirit'. Paul says this happened to all of us, past tense, no one excepted, at the moment we became part of Christ. Ephesians 4:5 lists 'one Lord, one faith, one baptism' as part of the unity of the church, which fits a single founding event, not a second one. Romans 8:9 says that anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to him. If a person could belong to Christ and still be waiting for the Spirit, Paul's words would not hold together. The strongest counterargument comes from the book of Acts. In Acts 2 the disciples receive the Spirit at Pentecost, days after they had already believed in Jesus. In Acts 8 the Samaritans believe and are baptized in water, and only later do Peter and John come and lay hands on them, and only then does the Spirit fall on them. In Acts 10 the Spirit falls on the household of Cornelius while Peter is still preaching, and in Acts 19 Paul finds disciples in Ephesus who had only known John's baptism, and they receive the Spirit after Paul lays hands on them. These episodes look like a second event after initial faith. The verdict handles the tension by reading the Acts narratives as describing what actually happened during the apostolic founding period, when the Spirit visibly extended from Jews to Samaritans to gentiles to incomplete-disciples to mark each new boundary being crossed, rather than as a step-by-step template every believer for all time must repeat. Paul's didactic letter to the Corinthians, which directly addresses the question of how all believers stand in relation to the Spirit, is the place where the doctrine itself is named. The tension does not fully disappear; honest readers feel it. Some Christians read Acts as the normative pattern and believe in a distinct later experience.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Christian lineages divide visibly on this question. The patristic tradition broadly tied the gift of the Spirit to baptism and the laying on of hands, with the Eastern church developing chrismation as the ritual moment when the seal of the Spirit is given, conducted immediately after baptism. Roman Catholics teach that baptism initiates a person into the life of grace and confirmation completes baptismal grace by giving the fuller outpouring of the Spirit; in their structure these are two stages but both belong to Christian initiation, not to a later optional crisis. Lutherans and Anglicans confess baptismal regeneration in their classical statements, treating the Spirit as given through baptism. Reformed and Baptist confessional traditions, including Westminster and the 1689 Baptist Confession, read 1 Corinthians 12:13 as describing what the Spirit does for every believer at conversion and treat the Acts episodes as transitional, not normative. Methodists historically taught a second-blessing entire sanctification experience after conversion, which the Holiness movement then sharpened into a Spirit-baptism event preceding the Pentecostal revival. Classical Pentecostals from the Azusa Street revival forward, including the Assemblies of God, the Foursquare Gospel, the Church of God in Christ, and the International Pentecostal Holiness Church, teach a distinct subsequent baptism in the Spirit normally evidenced by speaking in other tongues. Charismatic movements within Catholic, Anglican, Lutheran, and Reformed bodies hold a wide range of views that often soften the strict subsequence into a renewing experience compatible with conversion-Spirit-baptism. Anabaptist, Brethren, and cessationist Reformed bodies hold the conversion-only position firmly. The Plymouth Brethren and the dispensationalist tradition strongly affirm the conversion-only reading. This is an in-house Christian disagreement, not a boundary-of-the-faith question; Christians on both sides love the same Christ and confess the same gospel.

scripture.key_term: βαπτίζω baptizo G907 (ebaptisthemen, aorist passive 1st plural); ἐν εἷς πνεῦμα en heis pneuma G1722 G1520 G4151; σῶμα soma G4983; πάντες pantes G3956
scripture.force: The doctrinal locus on Spirit baptism. Paul says in one Spirit we all were baptized into one body. The verb is past tense, the agency is the one Spirit, the destination is the one body, the subjects are all believers (Jews and Greeks, slaves and free). This is the only verse in the Epistles that directly says what Spirit baptism is and to whom it applies. By Paul's grammar it is a corporate past event true of every believer at incorporation, not a future optional crisis.

scripture.key_term: βάπτισμα baptisma G908; εἷς heis G1520 (one, masculine, feminine, neuter forms); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; σῶμα soma G4983
scripture.force: One body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father. Paul names baptisma as singular and unifying, parallel to one Lord and one faith. A two-stage doctrine that makes Spirit baptism categorically distinct from conversion-baptism strains against Paul's deliberate listing of unities here.

scripture.key_term: βαπτίζω baptizo G907 (ebaptisthemen); Χριστός Iesous Christos G2424 G5547; συνετάφημεν sunetaphemen G4916 (buried with, aorist passive); θάνατος thanatos G2288
scripture.force: All who were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death. We were buried with him through baptism into death. Paul makes baptism the moment of union with Christ in his death and resurrection. He does not contemplate a later second-stage Spirit baptism that would be distinct from this union-with-Christ event.

scripture.key_term: βαπτίζω baptizo G907 (ebaptisthete); Χριστός Christos G5547; ἐνδύω enduo G1746 (put on, aorist middle, clothing imagery)
scripture.force: As many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ. Paul ties baptism directly to putting-on-Christ, which is the same as union with Christ at conversion. There is no two-stage gap between being baptized into Christ and being clothed with Christ.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; Χριστός Christos G5547; ἔχω echo G2192 (has); εἰμί eimi G1510 (is)
scripture.force: If anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ that one does not belong to him. Lexically airtight: possession of the Spirit and belonging to Christ are co-extensive. A person cannot belong to Christ and still be in a stage where the Spirit has not yet baptized him into the body. This verse directly settles the question of whether someone can be a regenerate believer without the indwelling Spirit; the answer is no.

scripture.key_term: σφραγίζω sphragizo G4972 (sealed, aorist passive); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; ἐπαγγελία epaggelia G1860 (promise); ἀρραβών arrabon G728 (deposit, guarantee)
scripture.force: In him you also, when you heard the word of truth and believed in him, were sealed with the promised Holy Spirit, who is the guarantee of our inheritance. Paul ties the sealing of the Spirit directly to the moment of believing the gospel. The Spirit-sealing is contemporaneous with faith, not delayed to a later crisis experience.

scripture.key_term: λουτρόν loutron G3067 (washing); παλιγγενεσία paliggenesia G3824 (regeneration); ἀνακαίνωσις anakainosis G342 (renewal); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151
scripture.force: He saved us by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit, whom he poured out on us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior. Salvation itself is described as a single Spirit-event tied to the washing of regeneration. The Spirit was poured out richly at the saving moment, not held back for a later subsequent stage.

scripture.key_term: βαπτίζω baptizo G907 (baptisthesesthe, future passive 2nd plural); πνεῦμα ἅγιος pneuma hagios G4151 G40
scripture.force: John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit not many days from now. Jesus uses the same verb baptizo with the Spirit as the element, addressed to the eleven apostles who had already been disciples for years. This is the foundational promise that the Pentecostal lineages cite as evidence that Spirit baptism is a distinct event after initial faith.

scripture.key_term: πίμπλημι pimplemi G4130 (filled, aorist passive); πνεῦμα ἅγιος pneuma hagios G4151 G40; γλῶσσα glossa G1100 (tongues); λαλέω laleo G2980
scripture.force: And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other tongues as the Spirit gave them utterance. At Pentecost the disciples, who were already disciples and had received the Spirit by Jesus breathing on them in John 20:22, received a public outpouring with audible and visible signs. The Pentecostal lineage reads this as the template for every believer's later Spirit baptism. The cessationist reading takes it as the founding event of the church age, not a normative repeatable template.

scripture.key_term: δέχομαι dechomai G1209 (received the word); λόγος logos G3056; βαπτίζω baptizo G907; ἐπιτίθημι epitithemi G2007 (laying on of hands); λαμβάνω lambano G2983 (receive)
scripture.force: When the apostles in Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent Peter and John, who came down and prayed for them that they might receive the Holy Spirit; for he had not yet fallen on any of them, but they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus. The Samaritans believed, were water-baptized, and only later received the Spirit through apostolic hands. This is the single most directly complicating Acts passage. The cessationist reading takes it as a deliberate apostolic verification of the Samaritan inclusion, parallel to Pentecost extending across a covenantal boundary. The Pentecostal reading takes it as a template for normative subsequence.

scripture.key_term: ἐπιπίπτω epipipto G1968 (fell upon, aorist active); πνεῦμα ἅγιος pneuma hagios G4151 G40; λαλέω γλῶσσαις laleo glossais G2980 G1100
scripture.force: While Peter was still speaking, the Holy Spirit fell on all who were hearing the word, and they were heard speaking in tongues and extolling God. Then Peter said, can anyone withhold water for baptizing these people, who have received the Holy Spirit just as we have? The Spirit fell on Cornelius and his household DURING faith and BEFORE water baptism, not after. This passage runs against the strict two-stage Pentecostal model on its own terms. It also runs against any view that ties Spirit-giving to water baptism as the necessary instrument.

scripture.key_term: ἐπιπίπτω epipipto G1968; βαπτίζω baptizo G907 (baptisthesesthe); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; ἀρχή arche G746 (beginning)
scripture.force: As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell on them just as on us at the beginning. And I remembered the word of the Lord, how he said, John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit. Peter himself reads Cornelius (Acts 10) as a parallel to Pentecost (Acts 2), and reads both as fulfillments of the Acts 1:5 promise. This is canonical interpretation of canonical narrative: Pentecost and Cornelius are bracketed together as the same kind of event (the Spirit crossing into a new boundary group), and the Acts 1:5 promise is treated as referring to these specific moments, not to a repeated lifelong template.

scripture.key_term: μαθητής mathetes G3101 (disciples); πιστεύω pisteuo G4100 (having believed); λαμβάνω lambano G2983 (receive); βαπτίζω baptizo G907; γλῶσσα glossa G1100; προφητεύω propheteuo G4395
scripture.force: Paul asks the Ephesian disciples did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed? They reply they had not heard there was a Holy Spirit, and had received only the baptism of John. Paul rebaptizes them in the name of the Lord Jesus, lays hands on them, and they receive the Spirit and speak in tongues and prophesy. The Pentecostal reading: a clear post-belief Spirit-reception. The cessationist counter: these disciples were not yet Christian believers in the full sense, they were John-the-Baptist disciples who had not yet heard the gospel of Jesus crucified and risen; their two-stage experience is a unique historical case of incomplete-disciples being brought into the New Covenant church.

scripture.key_term: ἐμφυσάω emphusao G1720 (breathed on, aorist active); λαμβάνω lambano G2983 (receive, aorist imperative); πνεῦμα ἅγιος pneuma hagios G4151 G40
scripture.force: And when he had said this, he breathed on them and said to them, receive the Holy Spirit. After his resurrection and before Pentecost, Jesus breathes the Spirit on the disciples. This makes Pentecost a public empowerment for mission, not the moment of regenerative Spirit-indwelling for these particular disciples. Complicates a clean two-event scheme on its own.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα pneuma G4151; δοξάζω doxazo G1392 (glorified); οὔπω oupo G3768 (not yet)
scripture.force: Now this he said about the Spirit, whom those who believed in him were to receive, for as yet the Spirit had not been given, because Jesus was not yet glorified. John's narrator-comment that the Spirit was not yet given pre-glorification anchors Pentecost as an epoch-shift, not a normative individual-believer's second stage. The Spirit becoming available to ALL who believe is the new covenantal reality post-glorification.

scripture.key_term: שָׁפַךְ shaphak H8210 (pour out); רוּחַ ruach H7307 (Spirit); כָּל kol H3605 (all flesh)
scripture.force: And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy. The OT promise that Peter quotes at Pentecost. The pouring out is one corporate eschatological event landing on all flesh in the last days, with multiple manifestations among the people, not a personal two-stage template per individual believer.

scripture.key_term: לֵב lev H3820 (heart); רוּחַ ruach H7307 (Spirit); חָדָשׁ chadash H2319 (new); נָתַן natan H5414 (give)
scripture.force: I will give you a new heart, and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will put my Spirit within you and cause you to walk in my statutes. The Old Testament promise of New Covenant Spirit-indwelling. The promise is one transformative gift at the moment of regeneration, not a two-stage scheme.

scripture.key_term: ναός naos G3485 (temple); πνεῦμα ἅγιος pneuma hagios G4151 G40; σῶμα soma G4983
scripture.force: Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own. Paul addresses the Corinthian believers as people who already have the Spirit indwelling them. He does not say some of them have it and others have not yet attained the second blessing.

competing_lens_verdicts.note: Reads 1 Cor 12:13 as the doctrinal locus binding on all believers. Reads Acts 2, 8, 10, 19 as transitional salvation-historical episodes marking the Spirit's extension across covenantal boundaries (Jews, Samaritans, gentiles, John's-disciples), not as a template for repeatable two-stage experience. Tongues and accompanying signs were apostolic-era authenticators, not normative.

Reads Acts 2, 8, 10, 19 as the normative pattern. The receiving-of-the-Spirit is a distinct experience subsequent to the new birth, evidenced by speaking in other tongues per Acts 2:4 as the inaugural template. 1 Cor 12:13 is read as describing the Spirit's general work in incorporating believers into the body of Christ, distinct from the empowerment-baptism modeled in Acts. The two are not the same event.

Reads baptism as initiation into Christian life and confirmation (chrismation in Eastern usage) as completion of baptismal grace with the fuller Spirit-outpouring. The Spirit is given at both stages of Christian initiation; the two-stage structure exists but is sacramentally located and is normally received within the church's catechumenal sequence, not as a later optional crisis.
Historically taught a second-blessing entire sanctification distinct from regeneration. The Holiness movement sharpened this into a Spirit-baptism for empowerment preceding the Pentecostal lineage.

hermeneutics.notes: 1 Cor 12:13 is the doctrinal locus; the Acts narratives are the locus for the alternative reading. The honest verdict requires choosing between (a) Pauline didactic primacy over Lukan narrative description, which yields conversion-Spirit-baptism for all believers and reads Acts as transitional, or (b) Lukan narrative as normative template, which yields a repeatable subsequent experience. Confidence is medium because the dispute is real at the level of which kind of NT material (didactic epistle vs descriptive narrative) carries normative weight on this specific question. Analogia scripturae heavily invoked: Rom 8:9, Eph 1:13-14, Tit 3:5-6, Eph 4:5, 1 Cor 6:19 all converge on Spirit-indwelling at moment of faith. Progressive revelation factor: Joel 2 and Ezek 36 promises land on the church corporately at Pentecost and individually at faith from that point forward; the corporate event was once-for-all (Pentecost), the individual application is one-step at conversion.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith Volume II, Sacrament of Chrismation, https://www.oca.org/orthodoxy/the-orthodox-faith/worship/the-sacraments/chrismation
counter_witness.key_phrase: If baptism is our personal participation in Easter, then chrismation is our personal participation in Pentecost. The newly baptized receives the seal of the gift of the Holy Spirit.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church, paragraphs 1302-1303 (Effects of Confirmation)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The effect of the sacrament of Confirmation is the full outpouring of the Holy Spirit as once granted to the apostles on the day of Pentecost. Confirmation brings an increase and deepening of baptismal grace.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession (1530), Article IX (Of Baptism), bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: Of Baptism they teach that it is necessary to salvation, and that through Baptism is offered the grace of God, and that children are to be baptized who, being offered to God through Baptism, are received into God's grace.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article XXVII (Of Baptism)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Baptism is not only a sign of profession and mark of difference; it is also a sign of Regeneration or New Birth, whereby, as by an instrument, they that receive Baptism rightly are grafted into the Church.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter XXVIII (Of Baptism), Section 6
counter_witness.key_phrase: The efficacy of baptism is not tied to that moment of time wherein it is administered; yet, by the right use of this ordinance, the grace promised is not only offered, but really exhibited and conferred by the Holy Ghost to such as that grace belongeth unto.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article XVII (Of Baptism)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Baptism is not only a sign of profession and mark of difference whereby Christians are distinguished from others that are not baptized; but it is also a sign of regeneration or the new birth.

Baptism shall be given to all those who have learned repentance and amendment of life, and who believe truly that their sins are taken away by Christ. The Spirit accompanies believer-baptism upon faith.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Articles 7 and 8 (Baptism in the Holy Spirit; Initial Physical Evidence)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The baptism in the Holy Spirit is a definite separate experience occurring subsequent to the new birth. With the baptism in the Holy Spirit come the initial physical sign of speaking in other tongues.

counter_witness.anchor: Charismatic-Renewal across Catholic, Anglican, Lutheran, Reformed bodies (1960s-present)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Spirit-baptism is a release of the Spirit already given in baptism, not a separate dispensation. Renewal language softens the strict-subsequence position into a deepening rather than a second-class divide.

counter_witness.anchor: Cyril of Jerusalem, Catechetical Lectures, Lecture XVII (On the Holy Ghost), CCEL NPNF Series 2 Vol 7
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Ghost descends on the soul to seal it, and the laying on of hands following baptism is the rite by which the Spirit is given to the baptized.

web.quote: 1 Cor 12:13: en heni pneumati hemeis pantes eis hen soma ebaptisthemen G907. Past tense, all believers (pantes G3956), one corporate baptism into one body by one Spirit.
Eph 4:5: heis Kyrios, mia pistis, hen baptisma. One Lord, one faith, one baptism. Singular baptisma G908 placed in a list of foundational unities of the church.
Rom 8:9: ei de tis Pneuma Christou ouk echei, houtos ouk estin autou. If anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, that one does not belong to him. Belonging and indwelling are co-extensive.
Gal 3:27: hosoi gar eis Christon ebaptisthete Christon enedusasthe. As many as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ. Baptism and union-with-Christ are one moment.
Eph 1:13: having believed (pisteusantes G4100) you were sealed (esphragisthete G4972) with the Holy Spirit of promise. Sealing tied to the moment of believing, not delayed.
Acts 1:5: humeis de en Pneumati baptisthesesthe Hagio. You will be baptized in the Holy Spirit. Future passive G907 spoken to disciples who had been disciples for years, the Pentecostal cite-locus.
Acts 2:4: kai eplesthesan pantes Pneumatos Hagiou kai erxanto lalein heterais glossais. Filled (pimplemi G4130 aorist passive) with the Spirit and began to speak in other tongues.

Acts 8:16: oudepo gar en ep oudeni auton epipeptokos, monon de bebaptismenoi huperchon eis to onoma tou Kyriou Iesou. The Spirit had not yet fallen on any of them; they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.
Acts 10:44: epepesen to Pneuma to Hagion epi pantas tous akouontas ton logon. The Holy Spirit fell upon all who were hearing the word. Spirit-reception during faith, before water baptism.

Acts 19:2: ei Pneuma Hagion elabete pisteusantes? Did you receive the Holy Spirit having believed? Paul's question and the Ephesian disciples' answer is the strongest text for the subsequence reading.

Baptizo G907: to dip, submerge, baptize. 81 NT occurrences. Used for water baptism (primary), Spirit baptism (Matt 3:11, Mark 1:8, Luke 3:16, John 1:33, Acts 1:5, Acts 11:16, 1 Cor 12:13), and metaphorical suffering (Mark 10:38).
Chrismation is our personal participation in Pentecost, the coming of the Holy Spirit upon us. The newly baptized receives the seal of the gift of the Holy Spirit, joined immediately to baptism.
